transcript: The team has special permits to approach humpbacks by boat and even swim close to the whales. Soon after sunrise, the first search begins. The team's photo and video researchers set out on the dawn patrol national geographic photographer flip nicklin records the whales above and below the surface he has no illusions that the whale's world remains largely unknown we're seeing such a small part of what the whale world is it's like trying to study elephants from underwater only when their nose is in the water and then wondering what elephants are doing the rest of their day. Flip's still photos are backed up by moving footage taken by fellow free diver Jason Sturgis. Flip and I pretty much do the documentation. We go out there, be the eyes for what's going on down below. Flip and Jason's mission is to gather background material to enable their colleagues to focus on the whales' behavior. When we come out early in the morning, we try and find whales that are going to be helpful for Megan or Jim. And before they get out here, we try and do the basics. We try and find them, find out what they're doing, get a photo ID, try and take just a tail picture. Thank you. And before they get out here, we try and do the basics. So we try and find them, find out what they're doing, get a photo ID, try and take just a tail picture. Each whale's tail is unique. Flip and Jason are adding to a photo database spanning 30 years to identify returning adults. White tail? White tail, yeah. Any animal that we run into, especially if we can sex it, if it's a female with a calf or something that by the situation we can tell you whether it's male or female, is really helpful for us down the road to know who they were, where they were, when they were, all those bits of information. Jason calls in the first situation report to Mekin. We are just off of Oluwalu and we are on 2 IDs, GPS and we are getting to down time right now. So we should have that by the time you guys get here. G'day guys, Yosti from Iron Edge here. Now I hope everyone has bunches of bananas worked into their nutrition plan because this edition of Basic Training is covering the monkey bars. With the epic first Australian Spartan sprint just finishing up, I was very surprised to see how many of our warriors actually incurred that dirt kissing and time wasting penalty of monkey bar failure, especially so close to the finish line. I guess, like many others, it was the unforeseen challenge of wet hands going into a grip-dependent obstacle that caused much of the problem. For me, I looked straight at the ground and wiped my hands on the grass as much as possible, curled my hands in a full two-handed grip around the bar, and used a slower one-hand reach, two-hand grab technique to complete it successfully. Yossi's advice for the day is always train and prepare for the worst and be constantly at the ready to react calmly and think laterally in the face of obstacle adversity. Rush, panic and scramble will inevitably mean you'll be doing every Spartan's favourite exercise, burpees. Without further ado, here are my four recommendations for battling the monkey bars. First up, do a little research and find a local park with a set of monkey bars to play around on. This will give you some specific practice and skills work. Start with a basic two-hand approach, getting used to the grip and the bars. Next, move to extending your reach and range, going with a single arm reach. This will help your rhythm and speed on the bars. Next, extend that reach out to cover more ground in less time. Once comfortable and confident, mix up your line and create a circuit to really challenge that specific endurance. Use chalk or gloves if you need to protect the hands. Naturally, if you slip off the bars, get down and give yourself burpees. Second to build that all important grip strength and strength through your back, thighs, shoulders and core is a grip force body row. Find a low bar or suspension trainer. Throw on your grips and set yourself up in a strong neutral position. Draw yourself up with a strong core and activated glutes and squeeze those shoulder blades together to activate those middle back muscles. If you feel your grip failing, reset so you don't slip but always complete those reps. If you have a cross core 180, smack out this combo. Next, to develop a strong rotational core connection between the upper and lower body and for creating great lower body drive for the monkey bars is a dead ball Russian twist. Remember, keep that chest up and spine neutral whilst elevating the feet and the legs. The further the ball is from the body, the longer the lever and the greater the demand that is placed on the core. Fourth is a bit of a nasty one called chin-up sevens, aiming to give you super monkey climbing strength endurance. Jumping on the Iron Edge chin-up bar, perform seven chins from a deep position to the half position. Next, punch out another seven from the half to the top position. Finally, as the arms begin to ignite with fire, hit seven full range. Keep a fire extinguisher on hand just in case. Here is a simple routine to help you get monkey strong for this obstacle. Also have a go at wetting your hands before you climb to get a real feel for what you might face going into the Spartan Race. Check us out at ironedge.com for all your equipment needs and stay up to date at the Iron Edge and Spartan Race Australia Facebook pages as well as the spartanrace.com.au site. As they carry out their morning sea search, they spot a possible lone male. As it dives, it creates a circle of flat water, a whale footprint. a strong vibration runs through the boat's hull. See any whales? I got him. I think I got him. I think he's here. They are right above a singing whale. Only male humpbacks sing, and it's thought only during the mating season. The whale appears comfortable with the boat's presence, so Flip takes the chance to observe the singer more closely. As a free diver, he doesn't use equipment that might spook the animal. The whale continues to fill the water with its song. When you're down there,'d just feel this tremendously loud sound. It's almost like being in a kettle drum. It was just the concussion. Less hearing it than feeling it. You're just getting pounded by the sound. And it hurt. And then going, you know what? Why are they they doing this why are they doing all this work flips photographs of the whales the first step now an expert in complex acoustic analysis is needed being the water was great but Jim is a guy is is the genius to figure out how do we measure this thing? How do we do something that not only we will understand and improve, but other good people in boats and other places where you can't be in the water can have this make sense? Jim Darling has spent three decades deciphering the code of the Hawaiian humpbacks. Initially researchers could only record life on the surface. Today an underwater microphone captures communication down below but still can't penetrate its mysteries. Whales know the ocean in ways Thank you for watching. Today an underwater microphone captures communication down below, but still can't penetrate its mysteries. Whales know the ocean in ways that we just can't possibly imagine. And yet it's our job as biologists, or at least as researchers, to try to imagine it. That's probably what makes it so compelling. We're trying to imagine something which is unimaginable in our land-dwelling human context. Jim can single out an individual song from the cacophony below. To sing, whales take a breath on the surface, then dive down about 15 metres. They hang almost motionless, head down so that the sound travels laterally. Air from the lungs vibrates vocal cord tissue that's controlled by a pair of articulated cartilages in the larynx. Looser tissue produces lower notes. The air expands a sac that may resonate the sound and transfer the vibrations to the water. Cycling air between the sac and lungs creates long songs, from high treble chirrups to low rumbling bass notes. Water's superb sound conductivity transmits the calls at over 1,600 meters per second for up to 160 kilometers. Other whales are blasted by the noise, picking it up with ears similar to ours through tiny slits near the eyes. The mechanism by which whales produce this most complex of all animal songs is now known. Understanding its purpose remains a bigger challenge. In his attempts to decipher the meaning, Jim spends every available hour out with the humpbacks. He can now recognize parts of the song's structure and can even predict what the whales may do next. He's into his buzzers now, so you'd expect him to be coming up any time. Whale song is complex because the animals use a wide variety of notes. They vary the scales, speed and intensity, and even deliberately repeat some parts. It's as you think about the song.
